perturbation.original_text: Therefore, the parties agree that, upon any breach of any covenant contained in this Section 3 by Shareholder, Heritage and HBC shall be entitled to an appropriate injunction for a violation of such covenant, threatened or actual, of such covenant, in addition to all other relief available under applicable law. If a court or arbitrator has determined that Shareholder has committed a breach by Shareholder of any covenant set forth in Section 3 of this Agreement, the Restricted Period will be extended by the period of the duration of such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, the parties agree that, upon any breach of any covenant contained in this Section 3 by Shareholder, Heritage and HBC shall be entitled to seek remedies as they deem appropriate. If a court or arbitrator has determined that Shareholder has committed a breach by Shareholder of any covenant set forth in Section 3 of this Agreement, the Restricted Period will not be extended.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit entitlement to an injunction and the extension of the restricted period after a breach weakens the protection for Heritage and HBC, potentially conflicting with California law regarding remedies for breach of contract and the enforceability of non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16601
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16601 allows for non-compete agreements in the context of the sale of a business's goodwill. By removing the specific remedy of an injunction and preventing extension of the restricted period, the modified text may limit the ability of Heritage and HBC to protect the goodwill they acquired, thereby undermining the enforceability of the non-compete agreement as contemplated by California law.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16601. Any person who sells the goodwill of a business, or any owner of a business entity selling or otherwise disposing of all of his or her ownership interest in the business entity, or any owner of a business entity that sells (a) all or substantially all of its operating assets together with the goodwill of the business entity, (b) all or substantially all of the operating assets of a division or a subsidiary of the business entity together with the goodwill of that division or subsidiary, or (c) all of the ownership interest of any subsidiary, may agree with the buyer to refrain from carrying on a similar business within a specified geographic area in which the business so sold, or that of the business entity, division, or subsidiary has been carried on, so long as the buyer, or any person deriving title to the goodwill or ownership interest from the buyer, carries on a like business therein. For the purposes of this
perturbation.justification: The original text provided specific remedies (injunction and extension of restricted period) for breach of covenant. The changed text removes the entitlement to an injunction and states the restricted period will not be extended, replacing the injunction remedy with 'seek remedies as they deem appropriate'. This directly weakens the protection against competition, which California Business and Professions Code Section 16601 permits in the context of the sale of a business's goodwill. By removing these specific remedies, the changed text undermines the enforceability of the non-compete agreement as contemplated by California law, as the ability of Heritage and HBC to protect the goodwill they acquired is limited. The scraped snippet confirms that non-compete agreements are permissible when selling the goodwill of a business. Therefore, the change introduces a legal contradiction by diminishing the means to enforce such an agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Process in any proceeding referred to in the first sentence of this section may be served on any party as required under California law.
perturbation.changed_text: Process in any proceeding referred to in the first sentence of this section may be served on any party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'as required under California law,' the contract fails to explicitly acknowledge and adhere to California's established rules for service of process, potentially leading to improper service and jurisdictional challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 415.10
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 415.10 details the requirements for personal service of process. Removing the reference to California law suggests that service could be performed in a manner not compliant with the code, potentially invalidating the service and any subsequent court action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 415.10 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 4. Service of Summons [413.10 - 417.40] ( Chapter 4 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) ARTICLE 3. Manner of Service of Summons [415.10 - 415.95] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 415.10. A summons may be served by personal delivery of a copy of the summons and of the complaint to the person to be served. Service of a summons in this manner is deemed complete at the time of such delivery. The date upon which personal delivery is made shall be entered on or affixed to the face of the copy of the summons at the time of its delivery. However, service of a summons without such date shall be valid and effective. (Amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 789.)
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures adherence to California law regarding service of process, while the changed text removes this explicit requirement. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight that removing this reference could lead to non-compliance with California Code of Civil Procedure Section 415.10, which details requirements for personal service. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 415.10, explicitly outlines the process of serving summons, reinforcing the importance of adhering to California law. The omission of 'as required under California law' directly undermines the original contract's commitment to following legally mandated procedures, making the contract potentially non-compliant, and therefore creating a legal contradiction as it relates to omission legal contradictions.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that either or both of them may file a copy of this paragraph with any court as written evidence of the knowing, voluntary and bargained agreement between the parties irrevocably to waive any objections to venue or to convenience of forum.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that either or both of them may file a copy of this paragraph with any court.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'as written evidence of the knowing, voluntary and bargained agreement between the parties irrevocably to waive any objections to venue or to convenience of forum' eliminates the explicit acknowledgment that the parties knowingly and voluntarily waived their rights to object to venue. This weakens the enforceability of the forum selection clause, particularly if a party later claims they did not understand the implications of the clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42 allows parties to agree that California law will govern disputes, which implicitly includes the right to choose a California forum. However, for such agreements to be enforced, there must be a clear understanding and waiver of rights. By removing the acknowledgment of a knowing and voluntary waiver, the contract opens itself up to challenges based on lack of informed consent, potentially undermining the intended effect of the forum selection clause. This creates a legal risk because a court might find the waiver unenforceable.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.42. (a) The following provisions of a contract between the contractor and a subcontractor with principal offices in this state, for the construction of a public or private work of improvement in this state, shall be void and unenforceable: (1) A provision which purports to require any dispute between the parties to be litigated, arbitrated, or otherwise determined outside this state. (2) A provision which purports to preclude a party from commencing such a proceeding or obtaining a judgment or other resolution in this state or the courts of this state. (b) For purposes of this section, “construction” means any work or services performed on, or materials provided for, a work of improvement, as defined in Section 8050 of the Civil Code, and for which a lien may be claimed pursuant to Section 8400 of the Civil Code (whether or not a lien is in fact cl
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly acknowledges that the parties knowingly and voluntarily waive their rights to object to venue, whereas the changed text removes this explicit acknowledgment. The Law Explanation states that 'for such agreements to be enforced, there must be a clear understanding and waiver of rights,' and that removing this acknowledgment 'opens itself up to challenges based on lack of informed consent, potentially undermining the intended effect of the forum selection clause.' This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, as the modified contract removes essential information related to the knowing waiver of rights, weakening the enforceability of the forum selection clause, and creating a legally questionable situation.
